slides.title: The French Revolution
slides.lead: How, between 1789 and 1815, France overturned a thousand-year-old monarchy and spread the ideas of liberty, equality and citizenship across the world.

media.alt: The blue, white and red tricolour flag of Revolutionary France beside a rising sun.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 180' role='img'><rect width='320' height='180' fill='#f4efe6'/><circle cx='250' cy='60' r='30' fill='#e8a13a'/><rect x='40' y='40' width='40' height='96' fill='#1f3a8a'/><rect x='80' y='40' width='40' height='96' fill='#ffffff' stroke='#d8d0c0' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='120' y='40' width='40' height='96' fill='#8a1f1f'/><rect x='34' y='34' width='132' height='6' fill='#5a4a2a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Open by asking what learners associate with 'revolution'. Establish that the French Revolution is a foundational event of the modern world: it challenged the idea that kings ruled by divine right and put forward the idea that power belongs to citizens. Preview the arc of the lesson: causes, 1789, the Rights of Man, the Republic and the Terror, Napoleon, and the Revolution's global influence.

slides.title: France under the Ancien Regime
slides.lead: Before 1789, France was an absolute monarchy ruled by King Louis XVI, who claimed to govern by the will of God.

blocks.text: The **Ancien Regime** ('old order') was a rigid society in which a person's rights depended on the group they were born into. The king held near-total power, there was no elected parliament, and ordinary people had little say in how they were governed.

items: France was Europe's most populous kingdom but was deeply unequal.
The monarch could imprison people without trial (by lettre de cachet).
Wealth, privilege and tax exemptions were inherited, not earned.

slides.notes: Define 'absolute monarchy' and 'divine right' clearly - the FET learner should be able to contrast this with a constitutional or democratic system. Stress that the problem was not simply a bad king but an entire structure of inherited privilege. This sets up the analysis of causes on the next slides.

slides.title: A society divided into three estates
slides.lead: French society was legally split into three 'estates' with very unequal rights and burdens.

blocks.heading: First Estate
blocks.text: The clergy (the Church). About 1% of people, but owned vast land and paid almost no tax.

blocks.heading: Second Estate
blocks.text: The nobility. Around 1-2% of people, holding privileges, top jobs and tax exemptions.

blocks.heading: Third Estate
blocks.text: Everyone else - roughly 97%: peasants, workers and the middle class (bourgeoisie). They paid most of the taxes.

media.alt: A pyramid divided into three layers, with a tiny top for clergy and nobility and a wide base for the common people.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 170' role='img'><rect width='220' height='170' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M110 24 L128 60 L92 60 Z' fill='#8a1f1f'/><path d='M92 60 L128 60 L146 100 L74 100 Z' fill='#c8b89a'/><path d='M74 100 L146 100 L176 150 L44 150 Z' fill='#7a8b5a'/><rect x='40' y='150' width='140' height='4' fill='#5a4a2a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Emphasise the analytical point: it was not just that most people were poor, but that inequality was written into the law. The bourgeoisie (educated, often wealthy middle class) resented paying taxes and being blocked from power by birth - they become key drivers of the Revolution. Ask learners to compare this legal inequality with apartheid-era classification they studied in earlier grades, while being careful not to flatten the differences.

slides.title: The causes of the Revolution
slides.lead: A financial crisis, an unfair tax system and bold new ideas combined to break the old order.

blocks.text: By the late 1780s the French state was **bankrupt**. Costly wars - including support for the American Revolution - drained the treasury, while the two privileged estates refused to pay tax. Bad harvests pushed up the price of bread, the staple food of the poor.

items: **Economic:** state debt, an unfair tax burden falling on the Third Estate, and rising bread prices.
**Social:** resentment of noble privilege and the ambitions of the bourgeoisie.
**Intellectual:** Enlightenment thinkers such as Rousseau, Voltaire and Montesquieu argued for reason, natural rights and government by consent.

media.alt: A balance scale weighed down heavily on one side by a loaf of bread and coins.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='106' y='30' width='8' height='90' fill='#5a4a2a'/><rect x='70' y='118' width='80' height='8' fill='#5a4a2a'/><line x1='60' y1='40' x2='160' y2='40' stroke='#5a4a2a' stroke-width='4'/><line x1='60' y1='40' x2='60' y2='78' stroke='#8a8577' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='160' y1='40' x2='160' y2='58' stroke='#8a8577' stroke-width='2'/><ellipse cx='60' cy='84' rx='24' ry='7' fill='#e8a13a'/><path d='M40 84 q20 -22 40 0 Z' fill='#d18a2a'/><ellipse cx='160' cy='62' rx='22' ry='6' fill='#c8b89a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Guide learners to distinguish long-term causes (structural inequality, Enlightenment ideas) from short-term triggers (bankruptcy, the 1788 harvest failure, the price of bread). This cause-and-effect distinction is a core FET history skill. Note that ideas alone did not cause the Revolution - they gave people a language to demand change when the crisis hit.

slides.title: From the Estates-General to a National Assembly
slides.lead: In May 1789 the king called the Estates-General; within weeks the Third Estate had declared itself the voice of the nation.

blocks.text: Desperate for money, Louis XVI summoned the **Estates-General** - an assembly that had not met since 1614. When the Third Estate was outvoted by the two privileged estates, its delegates broke away and declared themselves the **National Assembly**, claiming to represent the French people.

items: Locked out of their meeting hall, delegates gathered on a nearby tennis court.
In the **Tennis Court Oath** (20 June 1789) they swore not to disband until France had a constitution.
This was a direct challenge to the king's absolute authority.

media.alt: A group of figures with raised hands taking an oath together inside a hall.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='24' y='24' width='192' height='96' fill='#efe7d6' stroke='#c8b89a' stroke-width='2'/><g fill='#1f3a8a'><circle cx='70' cy='70' r='11'/><rect x='63' y='82' width='14' height='30'/><circle cx='120' cy='66' r='11'/><rect x='113' y='78' width='14' height='34'/><circle cx='170' cy='70' r='11'/><rect x='163' y='82' width='14' height='30'/></g><g stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='4' stroke-linecap='round'><line x1='70' y1='58' x2='78' y2='44'/><line x1='120' y1='54' x2='128' y2='40'/><line x1='170' y1='58' x2='178' y2='44'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Explain why the voting method mattered: by head or by order. Voting 'by order' let the two small privileged estates always outvote the huge Third Estate. The Tennis Court Oath is the moment sovereignty begins to shift from king to nation - a key conceptual turning point. Emphasise agency: this was a deliberate, organised political act, not a spontaneous riot.

slides.title: The storming of the Bastille
slides.lead: When crowds in Paris seized the Bastille fortress, the Revolution became a mass, popular uprising.

blocks.text: Fearing the king would use soldiers to crush the new Assembly, and angered by hunger and high prices, a crowd in Paris attacked the **Bastille** - a royal prison and fortress that symbolised the monarchy's power to imprison at will. Its fall showed that ordinary people could challenge the state.

items: The Bastille held weapons and gunpowder the crowd wanted for defence.
Its capture became the great symbol of the Revolution.
14 July - Bastille Day - is still France's national day.

media.alt: A stone fortress with round towers and a flag, surrounded by a crowd below.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><g fill='#8a8577'><rect x='70' y='40' width='100' height='80'/><rect x='60' y='30' width='24' height='90'/><rect x='156' y='30' width='24' height='90'/></g><g fill='#5a5750'><rect x='60' y='24' width='6' height='8'/><rect x='72' y='24' width='6' height='8'/><rect x='156' y='24' width='6' height='8'/><rect x='168' y='24' width='6' height='8'/></g><rect x='176' y='16' width='2' height='14' fill='#5a4a2a'/><rect x='178' y='16' width='16' height='10' fill='#8a1f1f'/><g fill='#1f3a8a'><circle cx='40' cy='132' r='6'/><circle cx='60' cy='136' r='6'/><circle cx='120' cy='134' r='6'/><circle cx='180' cy='136' r='6'/><circle cx='205' cy='132' r='6'/></g><rect x='24' y='142' width='196' height='6' fill='#7a8b5a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Clarify the symbolism: only a handful of prisoners were actually held there, so the Bastille mattered as a symbol of arbitrary royal power more than as a jail. This slide is a good place to discuss how symbols and emotions drive historical events. Note the shift from an elite political dispute to a popular revolution involving the people of Paris.

slides.eyebrow: 26 August 1789 - a new idea of rights
slides.title: The Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen
slides.lead: The National Assembly set out the principles of a new society: liberty, equality and popular sovereignty.

blocks.text: Men are born and remain free and equal in rights.
blocks.cite: Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen, Article 1, 1789

blocks.text: The Declaration stated that **sovereignty belongs to the nation**, not the king, and that government exists to protect natural rights. It promised freedom of speech, equality before the law and protection from arbitrary arrest - ideas that still shape constitutions today.

items: It abolished the legal privileges of the nobility and clergy.
It declared all citizens equal before the law.
Its limits: it did not give the vote to all, and women's rights were largely excluded - as Olympe de Gouges protested in 1791.

slides.notes: Balance celebration with critical analysis, which FET requires. The Declaration is genuinely revolutionary, and its language echoes in later documents - including the values in South Africa's own democratic Constitution and the Universal Declaration of Human Rights. But be honest about its limits: 'active' versus 'passive' citizens, the exclusion of women, and that France still held enslaved people in its colonies. Olympe de Gouges's 'Declaration of the Rights of Woman' (1791) is a good discussion point. [SME] Confirm the exact wording of Article 1 in the translation used by the school.

slides.title: The end of the monarchy
slides.lead: As the Revolution radicalised, France abolished the monarchy and executed its king.

blocks.text: The king resisted the changes and tried to flee the country in 1791. War with other European monarchies, who feared the Revolution would spread, deepened the crisis. In September 1792 France was declared a **Republic**, and in January 1793 **Louis XVI was executed** by guillotine, followed later by Queen Marie Antoinette.

items: The royal family's attempted escape destroyed trust in the king.
Foreign invasion and economic hardship pushed politics to extremes.
Radical groups such as the Jacobins gained power in the new Republic.

media.alt: A crown tilted and falling, with a break line beneath it.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><g transform='rotate(-18 110 70)'><path d='M70 88 L70 52 L88 70 L110 44 L132 70 L150 52 L150 88 Z' fill='#e8a13a' stroke='#b57e1e' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='70' y='88' width='80' height='12' fill='#d18a2a'/><circle cx='70' cy='52' r='5' fill='#8a1f1f'/><circle cx='110' cy='44' r='5' fill='#8a1f1f'/><circle cx='150' cy='52' r='5' fill='#8a1f1f'/></g><line x1='30' y1='128' x2='190' y2='128' stroke='#5a4a2a' stroke-width='4' stroke-dasharray='10 8'/></svg>

slides.notes: Trace the process of radicalisation: how war, fear and economic crisis pushed a moderate reform movement toward more extreme measures. The execution of the king was an enormous break - it declared that no ruler was above the law or the nation. Handle the executions factually and soberly; the point is the shift in the idea of sovereignty, not sensational detail.

slides.title: The Terror
slides.lead: To defend the Republic, the revolutionary government used mass arrests and executions - and turned on its own people.

blocks.text: Facing foreign invasion and internal revolt, a Committee of Public Safety led by **Maximilien Robespierre** took emergency powers. In the name of protecting the Revolution, thousands of suspected enemies were arrested and executed - many by guillotine - often after unfair trials.

items: An estimated 17,000 people were officially executed, and tens of thousands more died in prison or in regional revolts.
Fear was used deliberately as a tool of government.
In July 1794 Robespierre himself was overthrown and executed, ending the Terror.

media.alt: A tall guillotine frame against a plain background, shown as a stark symbol.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 200 170' role='img'><rect width='200' height='170' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='60' y='24' width='12' height='120' fill='#5a4a2a'/><rect x='128' y='24' width='12' height='120' fill='#5a4a2a'/><rect x='54' y='24' width='92' height='12' fill='#5a4a2a'/><path d='M72 40 L128 40 L128 64 L110 78 L72 78 Z' fill='#8a8577'/><path d='M72 64 L128 64 L110 78 L72 78 Z' fill='#6c6a63'/><rect x='54' y='140' width='92' height='8' fill='#5a4a2a'/></svg>

slides.notes: This is the most sensitive slide - handle it responsibly and without glorifying violence. The analytical question for FET learners: can a movement built on rights and liberty justify terror and executions to defend itself? Present the Terror as a warning about how fear and emergency powers can undermine the very freedoms a revolution claims to protect. Keep numbers factual and avoid graphic description. [SME] Verify execution figures against the textbook in use; historians' estimates vary.

slides.title: The Revolution at a glance, 1789-1815

events.label: Estates-General; Tennis Court Oath; storming of the Bastille; Declaration of the Rights of Man
First written constitution; the king's failed flight
Execution of Louis XVI; the Terror begins
Fall and execution of Robespierre; the Terror ends
Napoleon seizes power in a coup

slides.notes: Use this slide to consolidate the sequence before analysing Napoleon and legacy. Ask learners to identify the turning points and to notice the pattern: a hopeful beginning (1789), radicalisation and terror (1793-94), then a strong single ruler (Napoleon). This rise-and-fall shape is common to many revolutions and is worth naming explicitly.

slides.title: Napoleon: heir and betrayer of the Revolution
slides.lead: A brilliant general seized power, ended the chaos, and spread revolutionary ideas - while making himself emperor.

blocks.text: In 1799 **Napoleon Bonaparte** took power in a coup, ending years of instability. In 1804 he crowned himself Emperor. He preserved some gains of the Revolution - especially legal equality through the **Napoleonic Code** - but also ruled as a dictator, censored the press and built an empire through war.

items: The Napoleonic Code spread equality before the law and property rights across Europe.
His conquests carried revolutionary ideas - and resistance to them - far beyond France.
After defeat at Waterloo in 1815, monarchy returned to France, but the old order could not be fully restored.

media.alt: A bicorne hat and a law book, symbolising Napoleon as both soldier and lawmaker.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M40 70 q60 -34 120 0 q-60 22 -120 0 Z' fill='#1f3a8a'/><rect x='96' y='50' width='8' height='16' fill='#8a1f1f' transform='rotate(12 100 58)'/><rect x='60' y='96' width='90' height='48' rx='3' fill='#8a1f1f'/><rect x='60' y='96' width='12' height='48' fill='#5c1414'/><line x1='80' y1='110' x2='140' y2='110' stroke='#f4efe6' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='80' y1='122' x2='140' y2='122' stroke='#f4efe6' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='80' y1='134' x2='122' y2='134' stroke='#f4efe6' stroke-width='3'/></svg>

slides.notes: Napoleon is deliberately framed as ambiguous - this is a strong analytical prompt. Ask: did he complete the Revolution or betray it? He entrenched legal equality and merit, yet crushed political freedom and crowned himself emperor. Encourage learners to hold both truths at once rather than label him simply 'good' or 'bad'.

slides.title: The global influence of the Revolution
slides.lead: The Revolution's ideas spread far beyond France and continue to shape the modern world.

blocks.heading: Human rights
blocks.text: The idea that all people have rights simply by being human influenced later documents, including the 1948 Universal Declaration of Human Rights.

blocks.heading: Haiti
blocks.text: Enslaved people in the French colony of Saint-Domingue took up the language of liberty and, in 1804, won the Haitian Revolution - the first successful revolution by enslaved people.

blocks.heading: Anti-colonial struggles
blocks.text: The demands for liberty, equality and self-rule inspired independence and freedom movements across the world, including in Africa.

slides.notes: This slide connects European history to global and African history, as CAPS intends. The Haitian Revolution is essential: it exposed the contradiction in French ideals, showing that people France excluded took the Revolution's promises further than France itself. Draw a careful, non-anachronistic line to later freedom struggles, including South Africa's, where the language of rights and equality echoes 1789. [SME] Confirm the depth of the Haitian Revolution coverage expected at Grade 10 in the current CAPS document.

slides.title: Liberty, Equality, Fraternity
slides.lead: The Revolution gave the modern world a new political vocabulary.

blocks.text: Liberte, Egalite, Fraternite - Liberty, Equality, Fraternity.
blocks.cite: Motto of the French Revolution, still the national motto of France

blocks.text: These three words captured the Revolution's promise: freedom from arbitrary power, equality before the law, and solidarity among citizens. The gap between this promise and reality - then and now - is part of what makes the Revolution worth studying.

slides.notes: Use this slide to move from narrative to reflection. Ask learners to evaluate: to what extent did the French Revolution live up to its own slogan? This ties together the achievements (rights, equality before the law) and the failures (the Terror, exclusions, Napoleon's dictatorship) and prepares them for the review questions.

slides.title: What we learned - and questions to answer
slides.lead: The French Revolution destroyed an old order and launched ideas of citizenship and rights that still shape our world.

blocks.text: In just over a decade, France moved from absolute monarchy to republic to empire. It failed to fulfil many of its own promises, yet it permanently changed how people think about **power, rights and citizenship** - influencing revolutions and constitutions far beyond Europe.

blocks.question: Explain how long-term causes and short-term triggers combined to bring about the French Revolution in 1789.
Using the Declaration of the Rights of Man and the Terror, discuss the tension between the Revolution's ideals and its actions.
To what extent did the Revolution's ideas influence the wider world? Refer to Haiti and one other example.

slides.notes: Close by returning to the opening question: what is a revolution, and did this one succeed? The three questions mirror CAPS FET assessment styles - cause-and-effect analysis, source-based evaluation, and an extended 'to what extent' argument. Encourage learners to take a position and support it with evidence rather than simply narrating events. This lesson links forward to later topics on nationalism, human rights and 20th-century struggles for freedom.
